title: DBMS Assignments
text: DBMS Assignments DBMS assignments define the route to a database via a DBMS connector. During installation, two paths ($DBMS and $DBMS_DEF) are configured for default SQLite databases, but these can be replaced by path assignments to the actual databases used in your environment. Two-Path Structure Once you have defined a DBMS path assignment, you can direct other paths to it. For example, you could define a path to a MySQL database, and then assign the Repository paths to this new $MQL path: [PATHS} $MY_MQL= MQL:host:database|username|password $IDF= $MY_MQL $SYS= $MY_MQL $UUU= $MY_MQL $DEF= $MY_MQL Copy This two-path structure (path-to-connector, and then path-to-path) enables you to: Minimize the number of paths to the DBMS that are open at any one time. This can be important for a DBMS that has a limited number of open channels or logons available. Switch several paths to another DBMS or network with only one assignment, for example, by changing $DEF from $DBMS_DEF to $MY_SQL. Paths for Entity Data Each entity definition specifies a default DBMS to store data, and is assigned to one of the default paths that was created during installation, for example $DBMS_DEF. A typical working situation involves many types of users and situations, so you will often find that the default assignment in the entity definition is not sufficient. You can use assignments in either a local or a global assignment file to direct entities to another connector or to give another name to an entity. The paths used in these assignments can be paths that were established during the installation process or your own user-defined paths. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
